title: This Is the Right Moment; Can We Let It Slip Away?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked reading copy: the opening salutation omitted by OCR has been restored, damaged percentages and names have been verified against the printed pages, and clear OCR word-break and spelling errors have been repaired. The canonical source file remains unchanged. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: In The Times of India dated 2 November 2014, I saw a detailed research-based report on women's reservation under the headline “Empowerment of women remains on Paper: Report.” When the DMK came to power for the third time in 1989, it enacted equal property rights for women. In the same year, the DMK Government also enacted thirty per cent reservation for women in government employment.

Although every Government since Independence has proclaimed proper representation for women as a major goal, a Government report says women remain behind men. They have not been given due importance in shaping policy decisions or defining economic progress.

The Union Statistics Department's report Women and Men in India 2014 notes that only seven of the forty-five members of Modi's cabinet were women. Women ministers constituted ten per cent in 2004 and only fifteen per cent even ten years later.

Only sixty-two women won the 2014 parliamentary election. Women's participation as candidates rose from fifty-six per cent in the fifteenth general election to sixty-six per cent in the sixteenth; the corresponding figure for men rose from sixty to sixty-seven per cent. Of the elected women MPs, twenty were aged between forty-one and fifty; thirty-four per cent were social and political workers, and forty-four per cent held postgraduate degrees. Among male MPs, thirty-one per cent had an agricultural background, forty-two per cent were graduates, and twenty-nine per cent postgraduates.

In the States, women constitute only eight per cent of Legislative Assemblies and four per cent of Legislative Councils. The one remedy for the unequal share of public life and for giving women and men proper authority in democratic institutions is to guarantee reservation for women.

As I have said many times, each parliamentary session begins with an expectation that the Women's Reservation Bill will be passed, only for it to fail again for one reason or another. The demand for thirty-three per cent reservation in Parliament and State legislatures has been discussed for seventeen years.

Following a resolution at the DMK regional conference in Vellore in 2005, I called upon “Women of Tamil Nadu, unite,” and led a women's procession in Chennai on Anna's birthday, 15 September. On 6 January 2007, the DMK's high-level strategy committee resolved that women's processions be held in every district headquarters on 10 February to press for thirty-three per cent reservation. They were duly held.

At the DMK women's wing conference in Cuddalore on 14 and 15 June 2008, one resolution demanded legislation enabling women to contest thirty-three per cent of Assembly and parliamentary constituencies. The DMK has shown deep concern for this issue for many years and has never failed to pass resolutions at its conferences and general councils.

The issue was widely discussed when young leader Rajiv Gandhi was Prime Minister. As an initial step, the Panchayati Raj law made thirty-three per cent reservation compulsory in local bodies. Therefore, immediately after the DMK returned to office for the fourth time under my leadership in 1996, we held long-delayed elections to corporations, municipalities and panchayats. Of 116,747 representatives who assumed administrative office, 44,143 were women because the DMK Government reserved thirty-three per cent of all local-body posts for them.

The Women's Reservation Bill for Parliament and legislatures was first introduced in September 1996. Under the United Front Government headed by Prime Minister Deve Gowda, Law Minister Ramakant Khalap introduced it as the Eighty-first Constitutional Amendment. Opposition from the Samajwadi Party and Rashtriya Janata Dal sent it to a joint parliamentary committee headed by MP Geeta Mukherjee. Its report was presented to the Eleventh Lok Sabha on 9 December.

In the Twelfth Lok Sabha, the National Democratic Alliance Government reintroduced it on 26 June 1998 as the Eighty-fourth Amendment, but opposition protests forced adjournment. In the Thirteenth Lok Sabha under Prime Minister Vajpayee, Law Minister Ram Jethmalani introduced it again on 22 November 1999. Samajwadi and RJD members snatched the copy from his hand and tore it up.

The Bill was introduced once in 2002 and twice in 2003 but could not pass. In 2008, under the United Progressive Alliance, Law Minister Bharadwaj introduced it in the Rajya Sabha, where disorder again prevented passage. Thus the Bill was introduced under United Front, NDA and UPA Governments, but obstacles repeatedly blocked its path.
Why is a separate reservation law necessary? Can women not contest now? Does any law prevent them?

No law prevents them. A woman may contest for Parliament or an Assembly just as a man can. The Constitution does not stop her. Patriarchy does. How long can women keep fighting this entrenched patriarchal convention? If the law reserves a defined number of seats in which only women may contest, the foundation stone for securing their rightful share of power will finally be laid.

The system of reserved constituencies that constitutionally helps Dalit people obtain these rights at every level offers a model. Without disturbing that arrangement, organisations with social awareness and movements committed to justice must accept the long-standing demand for women's reservation and begin implementing it.

The argument that public opinion must be built is reasonable. But every attempt to build it is postponed for one reason or another until it becomes an indefinite wait. That must end. Efforts to enact the law must be pursued vigorously.
The previous Union Government sincerely tried to fulfil this long-standing demand and held both separate and collective discussions with all parties, yet could not reach a final decision.

Why? Farmers, workers, teachers and students generally voice their demands together. On women's reservation, parties cannot first unite behind a common voice. Some self-proclaimed progressives say women need no reservation. Some nod in support but impose conditions that unknowingly obstruct it. Others believe that whatever its form, opening the legal door to reservation is the immediate necessity.
No one need imagine that this last group opposes internal reservation for Backward and Most Backward women. Looking at their record of pursuing social justice would dispel that mistake.

The demand for internal reservation for women from Backward and Most Backward communities cannot be ignored. But our concern is that this demand must not become a dead end blocking the principal demand for women's reservation itself.

Even during the freedom struggle, the demand for communal representation was raised. After the Supreme Court rejected it, Periyar and Anna led the reservation struggle through the Dravidar Kazhagam and DMK. Following the appeal of Kamarajar, Prime Minister Jawaharlal Nehru brought India's first Constitutional Amendment in 1951. This historical precedent provides a path for securing further social justice for Backward, Most Backward and Scheduled communities.

Keeping that history in mind, all parties should first establish women's reservation firmly in law and then make arrangements for internal reservation. By giving women their rightful share of authority, India should show the world that it truly honours equality and social justice.

Parties supportive of women's reservation hold a majority in the present Sixteenth Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha. Remembering the sincere efforts under Prime Minister Vajpayee, Prime Minister Narendra Modi must recognise this favourable moment, refuse to let it slip away, and act with determination to enact the law.

The Union Government is working to pass sixty-seven bills during the winter session beginning on 24 November 2014, including controversial measures such as the Insurance Amendment Bill and labour laws. I trust it will show equal determination to pass the long-pending Women's Reservation Bill.
With affection,
M.K.
